macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Daf: Creditor as Watchman Over Collateral

micro_segments.title: Opening and context of the daf: collateral held by a creditor
The Mishna's position: creditor is a shomer sachar (paid watchman)
Rabbi Yehuda's distinction: money vs. fruit as collateral

macro_segments.title: The Baraita Debate: Rabbi Eliezer vs. Rabbi Akiva on Collateral

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's position: creditor is shomer chinam (free watchman)
Rabbi Akiva's position: collateral is for collection of debt
Case with a written document (shtar): all agree collateral is for collection

macro_segments.title: Rashi's Explanation: Memory Aid vs. Collection Purpose

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's rationale: collateral serves as a memory aid (zichron devarim)
Rabbi Akiva's rationale: collateral is held for payment of the debt

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Analysis: Reconciling Mishna with Rabbi Eliezer

micro_segments.title: Attempt to explain Mishna as consistent with Rabbi Eliezer: timing of collateral (at loan vs. after)
Gemara rejects timing distinction: both contexts use same language
Alternative explanation: Mishna discusses fruit; Rabbi Eliezer's baraita discusses money
Gemara rejects fruit explanation: Rabbi Yehuda's next clause shows Tanna Kamma applies to both
Gemara concludes: Mishna is not like Rabbi Eliezer (shomer sachar vs. shomer chinam)

macro_segments.title: The Role of Shmuel: Disproportionate Collateral

micro_segments.title: Attempt to frame debate: Rabbi Eliezer vs. Rabbi Akiva disagree on disproportionate collateral
Shmuel's extreme position: collateral represents the entire loan even disproportionately
Gemara rejects: all agree disproportionate collateral does not represent entire loan

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yitzchak's Principle: Creditor Acquires Ownership of Collateral

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yitzchak proves from Scripture that creditor takes possession of collateral
Significance of ownership: strict liability for loss vs. watchman status
Limitation of Rabbi Yitzchak's principle: only when collateral taken after the loan, not at time of loan
Gemara: all may agree to Rabbi Yitzchak; still need to explain Rabbi Eliezer vs. Rabbi Akiva debate

macro_segments.title: The Watchman Framework: Shomer Aviday (Lost Object) and the Debate

micro_segments.title: Parallel debate: shomer aviday (finder of lost object) as shomer chinam vs. shomer sachar
Rabba and Rav Yosef dispute: shomer aviday's status based on benefit from mitzvah
Rashi's explanation: benefit via 'osek b'mitzvah patur min hamitzvah' (occupied with mitzvah exemption)
Application to collateral: lender's mitzvah status depends on purpose of loan

macro_segments.title: Resolution: The Debate Hinges on When Creditor Needs the Collateral

micro_segments.title: Gemara: the debate is whether creditor needs to use the collateral for himself
Rabbi Akiva: even if creditor benefits from use, mitzvah status intact, thus shomer sachar
Rabbi Eliezer: creditor's self-interested use undermines mitzvah status, thus shomer chinam

macro_segments.title: Second Mishna: Moving a Barrel and Accidental Breakage

micro_segments.title: Mishna scenario: barrel is moved and breaks, whether watchman is shomer chinam or sachar
Tanna Kamma (Rabbi Meir): both types of watchmen take oath and are exempt
Rabbi Yehuda: shomer chinam swears; shomer sachar pays (logical position)
Rabbi Eliezer: astounded both can swear; questions whether either should be exempt

macro_segments.title: Gemara on Breakage: Negligence vs. Accident (Ones) Debate

micro_segments.title: Identification of Tanna Kamma as Rabbi Meir; interpretation of his position on negligence
Contradiction: Rabbi Meir elsewhere holds that tripping is negligent; here treats it as ones
Rav Elazar's resolution: two different versions of Rabbi Meir; need to distinguish contexts
Rabbi Yehuda's reasoned position: tripping is neither ones nor gross negligence
Rabbi Eliezer's critique: Rabbi Meir's position is extreme; questions when oath is valid

topical_tags.term: Osek b'mitzvah patur min hamitzvah (occupied with mitzvah exemption)
Rabbi Meir's position on moving barrels
